subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Grounds for Divorce Under DA s.8 — Separation, Adultery, Cruelty
fact_pattern: Thomas and Sandra separated on January 1, 2025, and have not cohabited since that date. Thomas files for divorce on January 15, 2026, on the ground of marriage breakdown. He states they have lived separate and apart for more than one year. Sandra does not dispute the separation date. No other grounds are alleged.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, has Thomas established a ground for divorce?

options.A: No — Thomas must also prove that the marriage is irretrievably broken by adducing evidence beyond the separation period.
options.B: Yes — under DA s.8(2)(a), living separate and apart for at least one year immediately preceding the determination of the divorce application constitutes proof of marriage breakdown, and the one-year period is satisfied.
options.C: No — the one-year separation period must be continuous with no resumption of cohabitation whatsoever, even for one day.
options.D: Yes — but Thomas must wait until two years of separation have elapsed before the divorce can be granted.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.8(1), marriage breakdown is the sole ground for divorce in Canada. Section 8(2)(a) provides that marriage breakdown is established by the spouses having lived separate and apart for at least one year immediately preceding the determination of the divorce application. Thomas and Sandra separated January 1, 2025, and the application is determined after January 1, 2026 — more than one year later. No additional proof of irretrievable breakdown is required beyond the separation period.
why_A_wrong: No additional proof of irretrievable breakdown beyond the one-year separation is required under DA s.8(2)(a). The one-year separation itself constitutes proof of marriage breakdown.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. DA s.8(2)(a) establishes marriage breakdown by proof of one year of living separate and apart, which Thomas has satisfied.
why_C_wrong: Under DA s.11(2), a period of resumed cohabitation of 90 days or less, for the purpose of reconciliation, does not interrupt the one-year separation period. Brief reconciliation attempts do not restart the clock, provided they do not exceed 90 days.
why_D_wrong: The statutory separation period for divorce in Canada is one year, not two years. There is no two-year requirement under the Divorce Act.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Grounds for Divorce — DA s.8
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(1), s. 8(2)(a)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Grounds for Divorce — Adultery and Physical or Mental Cruelty
fact_pattern: Natalie discovers her husband Adrian is having an affair. She wishes to obtain a divorce immediately without waiting one year. Her lawyer advises that adultery is a recognized ground for divorce that does not require a waiting period. Adrian has not admitted to the affair.
call_of_question: Which statement accurately describes the adultery ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)?

options.A: Adultery is no longer a ground for divorce in Canada since the 2020 amendments to the Divorce Act.
options.B: Adultery is a ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)(i); Natalie may allege adultery and, if proven, a divorce may be granted without the one-year separation period, but the standard of proof is balance of probabilities and Adrian cannot be compelled to testify against himself.
options.C: Adultery is a ground for divorce but requires a criminal conviction for the offence of adultery before it can be pleaded in divorce proceedings.
options.D: Adultery is a ground for divorce under the Divorce Act only if the responding spouse admits the affair in writing.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.8(2)(b)(i), adultery by the respondent constitutes marriage breakdown, which is a ground for divorce that does not require the one-year waiting period. The standard of proof is the civil standard — balance of probabilities — and corroborating evidence (direct or circumstantial) may be sufficient. The respondent cannot be compelled to testify against themselves as to adultery (DA s.10(4)). In practice, adultery cases are rarely litigated because the one-year separation ground is more straightforward.
why_A_wrong: Adultery remains a recognized ground for divorce under DA s.8(2)(b)(i). The 2019 Divorce Act amendments removed references to 'fault' in other areas but did not eliminate adultery as a ground for divorce.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Adultery under DA s.8(2)(b)(i) is a ground for immediate divorce; proof is on the balance of probabilities; the respondent cannot be compelled to testify.
why_C_wrong: No criminal conviction is required to establish adultery as a divorce ground. Adultery is proved on the civil standard of balance of probabilities in divorce proceedings, independently of any criminal proceedings.
why_D_wrong: A written admission is not required. Adultery may be proved by direct evidence or by circumstantial evidence sufficient to satisfy the balance of probabilities standard.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Grounds for Divorce — Adultery and Cruelty
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 8(2)(b)(i), s. 10(4)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Corollary Relief — Orders Available in Divorce Proceedings
fact_pattern: Karen and Paul are divorcing after a 10-year marriage with two children. In addition to the divorce itself, Karen seeks orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support within the divorce proceedings. Paul argues that property division cannot be addressed in the divorce proceedings and must be brought in a separate application under the Family Law Act.
call_of_question: Which of the following accurately describes the corollary relief available in divorce proceedings under the Divorce Act?

options.A: In divorce proceedings, the court may grant only the divorce itself; all ancillary matters such as support and parenting must be dealt with in separate Family Law Act proceedings.
options.B: The Divorce Act permits the court to make corollary orders for parenting arrangements, child support, and spousal support in the same proceedings as the divorce; property division under the Family Law Act is not available as corollary relief in federal divorce proceedings and must be pursued separately under provincial legislation.
options.C: The Divorce Act permits the court to make orders for all matters including property division, spousal support, child support, and parenting arrangements in a single proceeding.
options.D: Corollary relief is only available after the divorce has been granted; the parties must wait until the divorce is final before applying for support or parenting orders.

explanation: Under Divorce Act ss.15.1 (child support), 15.2 (spousal support), and 16 (parenting orders), a court may make corollary relief orders in conjunction with a divorce application. The Divorce Act does not confer jurisdiction over property division, which remains a matter of provincial law (Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I). Paul is correct that property equalization must be pursued under the FLA in a separate or concurrent provincial proceeding, not as corollary relief under the federal Divorce Act.
why_A_wrong: The Divorce Act expressly confers jurisdiction to grant corollary orders for parenting, child support, and spousal support in the same proceedings as the divorce. Karen's claims for these orders in the divorce proceeding are proper.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Divorce Act covers parenting, child support, and spousal support as corollary relief; property division is provincial and must be addressed under the FLA separately.
why_C_wrong: Property division is not available as corollary relief under the federal Divorce Act. It falls within provincial jurisdiction and must be pursued under the Family Law Act.
why_D_wrong: Corollary relief orders (for support and parenting) are regularly made at the same time as, or even before, the divorce itself. The court may make interim and final corollary orders without waiting for the divorce to become absolute.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Corollary Relief — DA ss.15.1, 15.2, 16
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), ss. 15.1, 15.2, 16; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part I

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Bars to Divorce Under DA s.11 — Collusion, Condonation, Connivance
fact_pattern: Gina and her husband Robert have agreed to allege adultery by Robert so that their divorce can be granted immediately without waiting the one-year separation period. Robert has not actually committed adultery, but both parties fabricate the allegation. The court is unaware of the arrangement.
call_of_question: What legal doctrine bars the divorce, and what is the court's obligation under the Divorce Act?

options.A: Condonation — because Gina has forgiven Robert's alleged adultery by continuing to live with him.
options.B: Connivance — because Gina has actively encouraged the alleged adulterous conduct.
options.C: Collusion — because the parties have agreed to fabricate or exaggerate the ground of divorce; under DA s.11(1)(b), the court must dismiss or stay the divorce petition if it finds collusion, and under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that collusion has not occurred.
options.D: There is no bar; the parties' private agreement about how to plead the divorce cannot be reviewed by the court once proceedings are commenced.

explanation: Collusion under DA s.11(1)(b) is defined as an agreement, conspiracy, or arrangement to fabricate or suppress evidence or to deceive the court in relation to the divorce proceeding. Fabricating an adultery allegation to avoid the one-year separation period is a classic example of collusion. Under DA s.11(1)(a), the court has a duty to satisfy itself that there has been no collusion. If collusion is found, the court must (s.11(3)) stay or dismiss the divorce application. Collusion is an absolute bar — unlike condonation and connivance, the court may not exercise discretion to proceed despite collusion.
why_A_wrong: Condonation involves forgiving a proven matrimonial offence (such as actual adultery), typically by resuming cohabitation after learning of the offence. Here, the adultery is fabricated entirely, so condonation does not apply.
why_B_wrong: Connivance involves wilfully encouraging or acquiescing in the respondent's actual matrimonial offence. Since the adultery in this scenario did not actually occur, connivance does not apply.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Fabricating a divorce ground to deceive the court is collusion under DA s.11(1)(b), and the court has a mandatory duty under s.11(1)(a) to inquire into and refuse to grant a divorce if collusion exists.
why_D_wrong: The court has an independent, mandatory duty under DA s.11(1)(a) to satisfy itself that no collusion exists in relation to the application. Private arrangements between parties cannot bind the court or exempt proceedings from this scrutiny.

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Effective Date and Finality of Divorce Decree — DA s.12
fact_pattern: A divorce judgment is granted by the Ontario Superior Court of Justice on March 1, 2026. Neither party appeals. Patrick wants to know when he is legally free to remarry.
call_of_question: Under the Divorce Act, when does the divorce take effect and when may Patrick legally remarry?

options.A: The divorce takes effect immediately upon the judge pronouncing the judgment on March 1, 2026; Patrick may remarry that day.
options.B: The divorce takes effect 31 days after the date of the judgment (April 1, 2026) unless an appeal is filed; after that date Patrick may remarry.
options.C: The divorce takes effect one year after the judgment; Patrick must wait until March 1, 2027 to remarry.
options.D: The divorce takes effect only upon issuance of a divorce certificate by the court registrar; Patrick must apply for the certificate before he can remarry.

explanation: Under Divorce Act s.12(1), a divorce takes effect on the thirty-first day after the day on which the judgment granting the divorce is rendered, unless an appeal has been taken. If no appeal is filed within 30 days, the divorce becomes absolute on day 31. Section 14 provides that after the divorce takes effect, the parties are free to remarry. The 31-day period is designed to allow for the filing of an appeal. A divorce certificate may be issued upon request but is not a prerequisite for remarriage after the effective date.
why_A_wrong: The divorce does not take effect immediately upon pronouncement. Divorce Act s.12(1) provides a mandatory 31-day waiting period before the divorce becomes absolute, designed to allow time for an appeal.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under DA s.12(1), the divorce takes effect on the 31st day after judgment if no appeal is filed, and the parties are then free to remarry under DA s.14.
why_C_wrong: There is no one-year waiting period after judgment. The Divorce Act prescribes a 31-day period from the date of judgment, not one year.
why_D_wrong: A divorce certificate is evidence of the divorce but is not a prerequisite for remarriage. Once the 31-day period passes without an appeal, the divorce is absolute and remarriage is permitted without a certificate.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Effective Date of Divorce — DA s.12
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 12(1), s. 14
